text: Thank you very much for the opportunity to come and visit Dublin to present some of the work we've been doing in Leeds on complete mesocolic excision. I'm Nick West, I'm one of the clinical lecturers in pathology and gastrointestinal pathologist based in Leeds working in the laboratory of Phil Quirk where we're trying to improve outcomes for patients with colorectal cancer by assessing the optimal surgical methods and feeding this back to surgical teams. Phil's done a lot of work in the nineteen eighties and nineteen nineties on rectal cancer and the importance of total mesorectal excision and the surgical grading of total mesorectal excision specimens and now we're trying to translate these same principles into mesocolic dissection for colon cancer. So the importance of TME is now well appreciated around the world. Bill Heald first published his experience of the importance of total mesorectal excision in the early nineteen eighties and we know that in countries where total mesorectal excision has been introduced as standard practice, for example here shown on the Stockholm data, we see an improvement in outcomes for patients in the order of around ten percent improvement in survival. Now at the same time Phil Quirk was rigorously assessing rectal cancer specimens and in nineteen eighty six he first published his seminal paper on the importance of the circumferential resection margin for rectal cancer showing that tumors within one millimetre of the circumferential margin predicted a higher rate of local disease recurrence. The next contribution from Phil was the introduction of mesorectal grading and Phil described a three-tiered grading system. We can see here on the slide on the left hand side the mesorectal plane dissection where the specimen has a nice intact mesorectal fascia in the TME plane. In the middle we've got the intramesorectal specimen where we see significant defects into the mesorectal envelope which don't extend down to the muscle tube. And then on the right hand side we've got the muscularis propria plane where we see major disruptions into the mesorectal envelope which extend down to expose the muscle tube. And Phil was able to show in the CRO-VII trial, shown on the right-hand side of the slide, the difference in local recurrence rates between muscularis propria plane excisions and the more intact specimens, with twice as many patients experiencing a local recurrence with muscularis propria plane surgery compared to the other two grades. And we've now got plenty of evidence that mesorectal grading is a worthwhile thing for pathologists to do and to feed back to surgeons.

The video shows two colon cancer specimens. The first specimen is short with minimal mesentery attached, displaying extensive tears down to the muscle tube. The second specimen has an intact mesocolon around the tumor, with cross-sectional slices showing the mesocolon extensively lined by peritoneum and no defects in the mesocolic envelope.

We know also from the literature that if we standardise our approach to colonic cancer dissection this will translate into an improved outcome for patients. So here we've got data from Warren Encker's group published in nineteen seventy nine showing that the five year survival for curative patients is in the order of eighty percent which is far in excess of other centres in this time period before the introduction of adjuvant chemotherapy. And they describe a very standardised approach to both colonic and rectal cancer surgery. A slightly later study published by Lesbocki and colleagues demonstrated that standardization of the approach for colon cancer at the time around nineteen eighty led to a fifteen percent improvement in survival for patients with colon cancer in their institution. And finally, we've got the data from the Erlangen Group in Germany, who have a very standardized approach to colon cancer dissection, including operating meticulously in the mesocolic plane with a central vascular ligation. And they've reported their outcomes on over thirteen hundred consecutive colon cancers, describing very impressive outcome data. including five-year disease-free survival of eighty-nine percent in all cases and seventy percent in stage three disease which is at least fifteen to twenty percent higher than that that we routinely see across the UK and also very low rates of local recurrence less than four percent. And when we look at the specimens from the Erlangen group, we can see clear differences between those specimens and routine surgery across the UK, with the Erlangen specimens more likely to be resected intact and with a high vascular ligation compared to the lower tie specimens from the UK. So we've developed a theory called the complete mesocolic excision theory, which is that a combination of the mesocolic plane with a high vascular ligation will remove the lymphatic and venous drainage around the tumour as completely as possible and should yield significant improvements in survival for patients with colonic cancer. Here we can see on the cross-sectional slice an early tumour which has not yet invaded beyond the muscle wall and deeper in the mesentery we can see two lymph nodes, one close to the bowel wall which appears to be normal and one further away which contains metastatic tumour and I'm sure you can appreciate that any deviations into the mesocolon in the region of the involved lymph node could potentially shed tumour cells into the peritoneal cavity and give rise to a local recurrence.

The surgeon demonstrates a mesocolic grading system similar to the mesorectal grading system, showing three grades: the muscularis propria plane with major defects extending to the muscle tube, the intramesocolic plane with significant defects not exposing the muscle, and the intact mesocolic plane with resection in the mesocolic fascial plane and an intact peritoneal surface. The microanatomy of the mesocolic fascia is detailed, highlighting the separation of parietal and visceral fascial planes during the CME approach.
The video shows a right hemicolectomy specimen with minimal ascending colonic mesentery attached. The attached mesentery is intact and covered by peritoneum, indicating a mesocolic plane excision.

The video shows a right hemicolectomy specimen with a defect in the transverse mesocolon, but the ileocolic mesentery, responsible for lymphatic drainage of a cecal carcinoma, remains intact. The defect is outside the drainage area, indicating a mesocolic plane excision. Isolated disruptions of peritoneal windows are visible, common in laparoscopic surgery, where fused peritoneum and mesocolic fascia lack intervening fat.

The video shows a laparoscopic dissection with visible disruptions around the central tie, some extending into the mesentery. A peritoneal window within a sigmoid resection is observed, where the fused fascia and peritoneum layer is perforated. Vessel separation is visible at the high tie, not extending into the mesentery proper, and lymph nodes are not exposed. A more exaggerated defect around the high tie extends into the mesentery, disrupting lymph nodes within the peritonealized mesentery.

We were able to independently verify the results of the LEADS study through the MRC CLASSIC trial where we had a hundred and sixty one gradable colon cancers and when we look at the distribution of the gradings we can see very similar to LEADS with twenty five percent of specimens resected in the muscularis propria plane and only twenty two percent of specimens being a mesocolic plane dissection. And when we followed up the classic trial patients and combined the mesocolic and intramesocolic plane groups versus muscularis propria plane, we can see a difference of around fifteen percent, which is similar to the difference we see in the LEAD study. And again, when you pull out the stage three cases, you can see twice as many patients alive at five years compared to surgery in the muscularis propria plane, which validates almost perfectly the earlier LEAD study where we see exactly the same association. One additional piece of information we gained from CLASSIC was the local recurrence data and surgeons often say that local recurrence for colon cancer is not an issue and very rarely occurs. Now we know from CLASSIC that surgery in the muscularis propria plane which happened in twenty five percent of cases is associated with a local recurrence rate in the region of twenty percent. So we can now say from Classic that surgery in the muscularis propria plane is associated with a significant rate of local disease recurrence and therefore we should be doing everything we possibly can to avoid surgery within this plane. The concept of central vascular ligation described in Erlangen is also practiced in other units around the world, including Japan, where the Japanese guidelines routinely mandate high-thigh surgery for advanced colonic cancer. The Japanese call this a D-III dissection and removal of the D-III central nodes around the high vascular thigh. and we know that the Japanese outcome data is similarly very impressive to the group reported in Erlangen and we can see here some data from Professor Sugihara from Tokyo showing again even in stage three disease very significant survival data which is vastly in excess of that reported in many UK institutions. And when we look at the specimens resected in Tokyo, we can see again, very similar to the German specimens, they're intact with a mesocolic plane dissection and high vascular ligations. But when we started to look at the photographs of Japanese specimens, we could immediately see they were different to German specimens in terms of the length of colon resected. And the Japanese principle is to operate ten centimetres beyond the tumour and then to transect the colon rather than the Erlangen principle of going beyond the next vascular arcade. Now we know it's not just Germany and Japan who undertake complete mesocolic excision with a central ligation. A number of surgeons across Europe and beyond practice this technique and have done for many years and are reporting impressive

Now the term complete mesocholic excision is appearing in a number of articles in the literature, some of which are very complementary towards this term and its principles and some of which are less complementary including this article published from here in Dublin by Des Winter and Hogan who described complete mesocholic excision and its novelty and the two authors said that twenty years ago when Bill Heald first introduced the term total mesorectal excision he was not inventing a new operation but had just proposed a new name for what was already accepted surgical practice and now history repeats itself for the colon. Now we agree with Hogan and Winter that complete mesocolic excision is not a new concept. Here you can see an article published in nineteen oh nine over a hundred years ago from a surgeon and an anatomist in Leeds where they described the lymphatic drainage of colonic cancer and the principle of resecting the mesocolon intact with a high vascular tie to adequately clear the lymphatic drainage. So it's certainly not a new concept, but what we would take issue with is that this is not routine practice. From our experience by examining specimens around the UK and wider around the world, we do not yet see standardised mesocolic plane surgery with a high ligation for all patients. There is certainly some work to do in standardising colonic cancer surgery. So what are the variables? Just to recap the three variables we see in colon cancer specimens. First of all there's the mesocolic plane, how well the surgeons have stuck to the mesocolic fascial plane and we know that this impacts on survival with the evidence coming from Leeds and the independent validatory evidence from Classic showing that mesocolic grading is associated with overall survival and from Classic showing an association with local disease recurrence. For the central ligation, we know that centres implementing central ligation as routine, such as in Erlangen and Tokyo, they report the best outcomes in the literature to date for colon cancer. So there seems to be some degree of additional benefit to high ligation, although the relative benefits of the mesocolic plane and the high ligation is yet to be deduced. Now the length of colon resected also differs between Erlangen and between Japan, but both groups are reporting similarly impressive survival. So it appears that the length of colon resected probably doesn't have the same influence on survival that the mesocolic grading and the high vascular ligation do.

The video shows a comparison of surgical techniques for colon cancer, highlighting the complete mesocolic excision (CME) with high ligation. Tissue morphometrical techniques are used to quantify the amount of tissue resected, including the area of mesentery and the length of bowel. The comparison between standard UK surgery and CME in Erlangen reveals an additional five centimeters of tissue between the tumor and the high tie in CME, along with a longer length of colon resected, resulting in a greater lymph node yield.

And we see the same pattern for left-sided tumours again with an extra five centimetres of tissue resected between the tumour and the high tie, additional length of colon, additional area of mesentery, more lymph nodes and more resections in the mesocolic plane. We've been able to use the same techniques to compare the German and the Japanese approach, and we can see here for right-sided tumours there's a very minimal difference between the tumour and the high tie, in the order of one point five centimetres for right-sided excisions. We can see the majority of the difference is in the length of the colon resectors, so the Japanese specimens are around nine centimetres shorter on average, with less area of mesentery and less lymph node yield, but a similarly impressive mesocolic plane resection rate. When we look at the left-sided specimens, no difference in the measurement between the tumour and the high-tie, but quite a significant difference in the length of colon resected, with an additional twenty-six centimetres of colon resected in the Erlangen specimens compared to the Japanese. And again, this is associated with more mesentery and more lymph nodes in the Erlangen specimens compared to Japan, but similarly impressive rates of mesocolic plane surgery. We've been able to now look at this in more detail in the stage three group with a follow-up paper where we've also been able to compare both Erlangen and Japan to the standard surgery in the UK, where we've taken Leeds as the example, showing that both the German and the Japanese specimens do take out more tissue between the tumour and the high tie, despite the differences between the two techniques in terms of length of colon resected. We've also worked with Robin Kennedy and Ian Jenkins at St Mark's in London looking at the value of laparoscopic complete mesocolic excision and compared this to the open approach in Erlangen and we've been able to show that in St Mark's using laparoscopic surgery the specimens are very very similar to open surgery in Erlangen with only a very small difference between the tumour and high time measurement for right-sided specimens

and no major differences in the area of mesentery resected and certainly no difference in the mesocolic plane rate. And for left-sided specimens, no difference in the tumour to high time measurements and similarly impressive mesocolic plane dissection rates. So we now know that laparoscopic CME can be effectively applied in the same way that open CME can. Finally, we've done some work in Denmark looking at the effect of multidisciplinary education on colonic cancer surgery and we were able to assess one of the hospitals, Hillerod Hospital, who had been to Erlangen to undergo colonic cancer training from Professor Hohenberger and implement this as standard practice for colon cancer in their unit compared to the other five hospitals in the Copenhagen region. We applied the same principles as the other studies by taking photographs of the fresh specimens and we were able to show that the Hilarod group, after switching their approach to CME, now resected more tissue between the tumour and the high tie, a greater length of colon, a greater area of mesentery, more lymph nodes and more likely to be resected in the mesocolic plane compared to the other five hospitals. And we saw the same effect both on right and left-sided resections. We've gone on to do some further work with Soren Lauberg in the Aarhus region of Denmark to show a similar effect that multidisciplinary education and surgical training can improve the quality of mesocolic plane excisions. So how do outcomes change following these multidisciplinary educational initiatives? We've now got early data from the Hillerod group that actually switching to a higher rate of mesocolic plane surgery is associated with improved survival. This data was published in Lancet Oncology in January of this year. and we can see that the Hilarod specimens were associated with around a ten percent better survival compared to non-CME specimens from the other hospitals across the region. Since this data was collected the Danish governments have funded a national training program in colonic cancer and all of the hospitals within the Copenhagen region of Denmark have undergone surgical training and you can see on the pie charts there is now a difference in mesocolic grading from before the regional education program across the whole region where we've seen the number of mesocolic plane excisions improve and the number of intramesocolic plane excisions reduce, and this should therefore translate into an improvement in outcome for patients.

We've got some ongoing studies across the world, one of which is a UK study, the FOXTROC trial, where we're looking at the value of preoperative chemotherapy for advanced colon cancer, and this may yield additional survival benefits in addition to standardising the mesocolic grading. We're also doing some educational initiatives within the UK, starting with a pilot programme in the Yorkshire region funded by Yorkshire Cancer Research, where we've brought all of the local surgeons and pathologists together to study the evidence and to try and improve the number of mesocolic plane excisions across the region. We're also involved in the T-REX study which is being run through Professor Sugihara in Japan where we're analyzing the differences between the European approach of taking an extended length of colon versus the Japanese approach of taking a very short length of colon to understand the differences in lymph node yields and the distribution of positive lymph nodes in order to understand which one of these two techniques is optimal. And finally, we're undertaking the GLSEN trial, which is a collaboration between Leeds and the Mater Hospital here in Dublin, looking at the value of intraoperative fluorescence to identify positive lymph nodes and therefore stratify the operative approach, with the concept being that positive disease in the mesentery may undergo a high tie whereas negative disease will undergo a lower level ligation to try and reduce the morbidity which has been reported in some units for complete mesocolic excision with a central ligation. So just to summarize I've shown you that there is significant variation in the quality of colon cancer specimens and there is significant variation in colon cancer outcomes and I think there is good evidence that the two are linked. We've shown that the plane of surgery is important and the evidence for this comes from the lead study and validated by the classic trial with mesocolic plane surgery having a better outcome and this is exaggerated in stage three disease. We know that the addition of complete musicolic excision with central vascular ligation appears to be associated with additional benefits and the evidence for this comes from the impressive survivals reported in Erlangen and in Japan when compared to the rest of the world. There's evidence from Denmark that multidisciplinary education is beneficial. It certainly improves the quality of specimens and we've demonstrated that in Hillerod and then wider across the region. We've also now seen the longer-term survival improvements in Hillerod compared to the other hospitals. We know that laparoscopic surgery can effectively produce the same high quality specimen to open surgery and we've demonstrated this through the work we've done with Robin and Ian at St Mark's.

And finally, pathological quality control is the process that underpins all of these developments and drives forward the improvement in surgery and therefore improvements in outcomes for patients. So we need to encourage greater collaboration between surgeons and pathologists with routine pathological feedback by taking photographs of specimens after resection and the demonstration of any defects at the multidisciplinary team meetings. So we now routinely photograph all of our specimens as they come into the department, preferably fresh, although formerly in fixed specimen photographs are perfectly acceptable, with additional images taken of the cross-sectional slices, and we will try and show these in the multidisciplinary team meetings to discuss any issues with the mesocolic gradings. So I just want to finish with this quote from the eminent UK pathologist Cuthbert Dukes who recognised the importance of high quality surgery very early on during his practice and Dukes said, I should not choose the operation but I should choose the surgeon to do it and I should choose him with great care. and I think that makes complete sense. The surgeon is very important in the cure of patients with colonic cancer and if they do a good job and resect the specimen in the mesocolic plane then the patient is more likely to survive and we've had the pleasure of working with a number of surgeons from the UK, across Europe and beyond over the years who've enabled us to do our work on mesocolic grading and tissue morphometry to identify the optimal techniques which are associated with the best outcomes for patients. But I think the one thing that Dukes forgot to mention is that one should also choose the right pathologist because if we don't identify any defects in the specimen and feed this back to the team to aid their learning, if we don't identify all of the lymph nodes and all of the extramural vascular invasion, the peritoneal disease and the margin positivity from which patients may benefit from adjuvant therapy, then we're doing our patients a disservice. So we need further collaborations between pathology and surgeons to improve outcomes for our patients. So I'd just like to thank all of our local, our national and our international collaborators who contributed to the data presented. And at the bottom, I need to acknowledge our funders, Yorkshire Cancer Research, the National Institute for Health Research, the Pathological Society and the Academy of Medical Sciences for funding the work we do in Leeds.
